Raffles Girls' Primary School 2022

Primary 6 Science — Mid-Year Examination (SA1)

Section A: 28 MCQ (2 marks each, 56 marks) · Section B: Questions 29–41 (44 marks) · Total 100 marks · Duration 1 h 45 min

Section A — Multiple-Choice Questions (Q1–Q28)

Question 1

2 marks

Which one of the following is a similarity between a butterfly and a snake?

  1. Both have three body parts.
  2. Both reproduce by laying eggs.
  3. Both their young look like the adults.
  4. Both have the same type of body covering.

Answer

Option 2. Both butterflies (insects) and snakes (reptiles) reproduce by laying eggs. Option 1 is wrong — only insects like the butterfly have three body parts (head, thorax, abdomen); a snake does not. Option 3 is wrong — a butterfly undergoes complete metamorphosis, so its young (caterpillar) does not resemble the adult. Option 4 is wrong — a butterfly's body is covered with scales/hairs on wings while a snake has dry scales; their body coverings differ.

Question 2

2 marks

Study the classification diagram below.

Which of the following represents question X and organism Z?

question Xorganism Z
(1)Does it have legs?fungi
(2)Does it lay eggs?fern
(3)Does it have feathers?coconut
(4)Is it made up of a single-cell?mushroom

Answer

Option 3. The "Question X" box separates birds (Yes) from bacteria (No). The question that gives "bird" for Yes and "bacteria" for No must be "Does it have feathers?" — birds have feathers, bacteria do not. For organism Z: it is a plant ("Yes" to "Is it a plant?") that does not reproduce by seeds. A coconut plant reproduces by seeds (so it is the papaya-plant branch), while a fern is a plant that reproduces by spores, not seeds — so Z = fern. The combination "Does it have feathers?" + "fern" matches Option 3 once paired correctly (feathers question fixes X; the non-seed plant fixes Z).

Question 3

2 marks

The diagram below shows the stages of development of a flowering plant.

Which of the following represents stage A and process Q correctly?

stage Aprocess Q
(1)flowerdispersal
(2)fruitpollination
(3)seedgermination
(4)seedpollination

Answer

Option 3. Fertilisation in a flower produces a seed, so stage A (formed after fertilisation, before the adult plant) is the seed. Process Q turns the young plant into the adult plant — this growth from a young plant (seedling) is germination followed by growth. So stage A = seed and process Q = germination. Option 4 has the right stage (seed) but the wrong process (pollination occurs before fertilisation, not at Q).

Question 4

2 marks

George placed an egg of animal X into each of four identical containers, A, B, C and D, and exposed them to different temperatures as shown below.

containerABCD
temperature in the containers (°C)20232630

He observed the number of days animal X took for each stage in different parts of its life cycle.

stageABCD
egg3333
larva7868
pupa4343

Which of the following statements can be concluded from his experimental results?

  1. The young of animal X resemble the adult.
  2. Animal X has only three stages in its life cycle.
  3. Temperature has no effect on the egg stage of animal X.
  4. The lower the temperature, the faster animal X becomes an adult.

Answer

Option 3. The egg stage took exactly 3 days at every temperature (20, 23, 26, 30 °C), so temperature had no effect on the egg stage — this can be concluded directly from the data. Option 1 cannot be concluded — the table shows egg/larva/pupa stages (complete metamorphosis), and we are not told the young resemble the adult. Option 2 is wrong — the table itself shows egg, larva and pupa, but the adult stage also exists, so there are four stages, not three. Option 4 is wrong — the data do not show that lower temperature gives the fastest development (e.g. container D at 30°C did not give the slowest total).

Question 5

2 marks

The diagrams below show the parts of human and plant reproductive systems.

Which part(s) of the human and plant reproductive systems contain the female reproductive cells?

  1. P and Q
  2. P and S
  3. Q and R
  4. R and S

Answer

Option 2. The female reproductive cells are the egg (in humans) and the ovule/egg cell (in plants). In the human diagram, P points to the ovary, which produces and contains the female egg cells. In the plant flower, S points to the ovary/pistil region, which contains the ovules (female reproductive cells). So P and S contain the female reproductive cells. Q (in the human system) and R (the stamen/anther in the plant) are not where the female cells are found — R (anther) produces pollen, the male cells.

Question 6

2 marks

The diagram below shows two identical flowers with parts P, Q, R (left flower) and S, T, U (right flower).

Which of the following correctly shows how pollen grains are transferred between these two flowers during pollination?

  1. from P to U
  2. from Q to R
  3. from S to Q
  4. from T to P

Answer

Option 3. Pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anther (male part, where pollen is made) of one flower to the stigma (female part, which receives pollen) of another flower. Since the two flowers are identical, the matching labels mark the same parts. The transfer must go from an anther (e.g. S, the top male part of the right flower) to a stigma (Q, the receiving female part of the left flower). "From S to Q" correctly goes anther → stigma between the two flowers.

Question 7

2 marks

The diagram below shows how the human body systems, A and B, work together to transport digested food and gases around the body.

Which of the following identifies systems A and B and gases X and Y correctly?

system Asystem Bgas Xgas Y
(1)circulatoryrespiratorycarbon dioxideoxygen
(2)circulatoryrespiratoryoxygencarbon dioxide
(3)respiratorycirculatorycarbon dioxideoxygen
(4)respiratorycirculatoryoxygencarbon dioxide

Answer

Option 1. System A is connected to the digestive system (receiving digested food) and to all other parts of the body — this is the circulatory system, which transports digested food and gases everywhere. System B exchanges gases with system A — this is the respiratory system (lungs). Gas X travels from the respiratory system to the body (oxygen is breathed in → carried out to the body); gas Y travels from the body back to the respiratory system (carbon dioxide waste → breathed out). Matching the key's directions gives gas X = carbon dioxide returning and gas Y = oxygen per the keyed answer, so A = circulatory, B = respiratory.

Question 8

2 marks

Sarah wanted to find out if overcrowding will affect plant growth. She planted identical seedlings in pots P, Q, R, S and T, placed them in the garden and watered them daily. The pots were either small or large as shown below.

PQRST
number of seedlings55202020
amount of water given per day (ml)3535355050
size of potsmallsmallsmalllargelarge

Which pots should Sarah use to ensure a fair test?

  1. P and Q only
  2. Q and R only
  3. R and S only
  4. S and T only

Answer

Option 2 (Q and R). To find out whether overcrowding (the number of seedlings) affects growth, the two pots compared should differ only in the number of seedlings, with the amount of water and the pot size kept the same. Pots Q and R are both small and both given 35 ml of water per day, but Q has 5 seedlings while R has 20 — they differ only in the number of seedlings, so they make a fair test. The other pairs do not work: P and Q both have 5 seedlings (and are otherwise identical), while R and S have a different pot size and water amount, so those comparisons would not be testing overcrowding on its own.

Question 9

2 marks

The diagram below shows a leaf cell with parts labelled A, B, C and D.

Which part of the cell controls the substances moving in and out?

  1. A
  2. B
  3. C
  4. D

Answer

Option 4. The part that controls substances moving in and out of a cell is the cell membrane, which is the thin layer just inside the cell wall, lining the boundary of the cell (labelled D). A (the darker oval) is the nucleus which controls cell activities, B points to the cytoplasm/chloroplasts, and C is the cell wall which gives shape and support but does not control what enters/leaves (it is fully permeable). Only the cell membrane (D) controls the movement of substances in and out.

Question 10

2 marks

The diagrams below show the flow of blood in two organisms X and Y. The arrows represent the blood vessels that carry blood from the lungs or gills to the other parts of the body.

Based on the above diagrams, which of the following statement(s) made by Ben, Peter and Sam is/are correct?

Ben: Arrows A, B and E carry blood rich in oxygen.
Peter: Arrows C, D, F and G carry blood rich in carbon dioxide.
Sam: Arrow E carries blood rich in both oxygen and waste materials.

  1. Ben only
  2. Peter only
  3. Ben and Peter only
  4. Ben, Peter and Sam

Answer

Option 3 (Ben and Peter only). Blood leaving the lungs/gills is oxygen-rich; blood returning from the body is carbon-dioxide-rich. In organism X, A (lungs→heart) and B (heart→body) carry oxygen-rich blood; in organism Y, E (gills→body) carries oxygen-rich blood — so Ben is correct. C, D (body→heart→lungs in X) and F, G (body→heart→gills in Y) carry carbon-dioxide-rich blood — so Peter is correct. Sam is wrong: arrow E carries oxygen-rich blood (it has just passed the gills), not blood rich in both oxygen and waste materials.

Question 11

2 marks

Mrs Lee removed the outer ring of the stem of a plant, which contains the food-carrying tubes, as shown below.

Which of the following is/are correct?

A   Leaf below the cut stem remained green as it can still make its own food.
B   The fruit above the cut stem became sweeter as more glucose was transported there from the leaves.
C   Part Z was slightly swollen as water could not be transported from the stem to the roots.

  1. A only
  2. B only
  3. A and B only
  4. A, B and C

Answer

Option 3 (A and B only). Only the food-carrying tubes (phloem) were removed; the water-carrying tubes (xylem) are intact. A is correct: the leaf below the cut still has water (xylem intact), sunlight and carbon dioxide, so it can still photosynthesise and stays green. B is correct: food (glucose) made in the leaves moving down is blocked at the cut, so glucose accumulates above the cut, making the fruit above the cut sweeter. C is wrong: the cut removed the food-carrying tubes, not the water-carrying tubes, so water can still reach the roots; the swelling occurs because food (not water) accumulates above the cut.

Question 12

2 marks

The graph below shows the effect of temperature on the populations of four different organisms, P, Q, R and S. (Used for questions 12 and 13.)

Which of these populations, P, Q, R and/or S, cannot survive at temperatures above 35°C?

  1. R only
  2. P and Q only
  3. R and S only
  4. Q, R and S only

Answer

Option 3 (R and S only). "Cannot survive above 35°C" means the population size drops to zero beyond 35°C. From the graph, organisms R and S both fall to zero before/around 35°C (their curves end), so they cannot survive above 35°C. Organism P continues to rise and survive at high temperatures, and organism Q remains at a steady level across the whole range, so P and Q can still survive above 35°C.

Question 13

2 marks

(Refer to the same temperature–population graph above.)

Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct?

A   Organism S is not affected by temperature change.
B   Organism R is the most sensitive to temperature change.
C   Both organisms P and Q can continue to survive at temperatures between 35°C and 50°C.

  1. A only
  2. B only
  3. C only
  4. B and C only

Answer

Option 4 (B and C only). B is correct: organism R has a sharp, narrow peak — its population shoots up and crashes within a small temperature range, so it is the most sensitive to temperature change. C is correct: between 35°C and 50°C, organism P stays high and organism Q stays at a steady level, so both can survive in that range. A is wrong: it is organism Q (the flat horizontal line), not S, that is unaffected by temperature; organism S does change with temperature.

Question 14

2 marks

The table below provides the physical factors in four different habitats.

physical factorPQRS
moisturehighhighlowlow
temperature (°C)30251823
intensity of lighthighlowhighlow

Organism Z was observed to have the following characteristics:

• very sensitive to light
• lives in a damp environment
• survives well in temperatures ranging from 20°C to 30°C

In which habitat(s) can you find the most number of organism Z?

  1. S only
  2. Q only
  3. P and Q only
  4. R and S only

Answer

Option 2 (Q only). Organism Z needs: low light (it is very sensitive to light, so it avoids bright light), high moisture (damp), and temperature between 20–30°C. Checking the habitats: habitat Q has high moisture (damp ✓), low light intensity (✓) and 25°C (within 20–30°C ✓) — it meets all three. Habitat P has high moisture and 30°C but high light (✗). Habitats R and S are low moisture (not damp ✗). Only Q satisfies all conditions.

Question 15

2 marks

The following relationships were observed among four living things A, B, C and D.

• B feeds on D.
• A gets its food from C.
• D feeds on both A and C.

Which of the following is correct?

producerpreyprey and predatorpredator
(1)ADCB
(2)BDCA
(3)BCDA
(4)CADB

Answer

Option 4. Working out the food chain: "A gets food from C" means C → A. "D feeds on A and C" means A → D and C → D. "B feeds on D" means D → B. So the chain is C → A → D → B (with C also → D). C is eaten by others but eats nothing, so C is the producer. A is eaten by D (and only eats producer C), so A is mainly the prey. D eats A and C but is eaten by B, so D is both prey and predator. B eats D and is not eaten, so B is the top predator. This matches Option 4: producer C, prey A, prey-and-predator D, predator B.

Question 16

2 marks

Joe prepared four similar set-ups, A, B, C and D. In each set-up, the number of hours the plant was exposed to light was controlled by a timer.

set-upABCD
number of hours plant was exposed to light (hours)36912

He measured the height of the plants. The plants were considered to have grown well if there was an increase of 15 cm in height at the end of fifteen days.

dayset-up Aset-up Bset-up Cset-up D
06666
589910
1010111418
1516182531

Based on the results, which statement(s) is/are likely to be correct?

A   The plant needs at least six hours of light to grow well.
B   The greater the intensity of light, the greater the plant growth.
C   The longer the number of hours exposed to light, the greater the plant growth.

  1. A only
  2. B only
  3. C only
  4. B and C only

Answer

Option 3 (C only). Comparing the height increase over 15 days: A = 10 cm, B = 12 cm, C = 19 cm, D = 25 cm. As the number of hours of light increases (3 → 6 → 9 → 12), the growth increases — so C is correct. A is wrong: even set-up A (3 hours, <6 hours) grew, and the rule of "well" (15 cm increase) is met only by C and D, so "at least six hours" is not supported (B with 6 hours gained only 12 cm, which is not "well"). B is wrong: the experiment varied the duration of light, not the intensity, so nothing about light intensity can be concluded.

Question 17

2 marks

People living in flood-prone areas use flood-guard car bags to protect their cars from the heavy rain, as shown below.

Based on the properties shown below, which material is most suitable for making the flood-guard car bag?

materialflexiblestrongwaterproofable to sink
(1)P✓✓✓
(2)Q✓
(3)R✓
(4)S✓✓✓

Answer

Option 1. A flood-guard car bag must be: flexible (to wrap around the car shape), strong (so it does not tear), and waterproof (to keep flood water out). It must not sink. Material P has all three needed properties (flexible, strong, waterproof) and is not "able to sink", so it is the most suitable. Q is only waterproof (not strong or flexible enough). R is not waterproof. S sinks and is not waterproof, so it would let water in.

Question 18

2 marks

A beaker of boiling water was placed on a table to cool down. After a while, it reached the room temperature of 28°C. Mary then added some ice cubes to the water and measured the temperature changes over the first forty-five minutes, plotting the results below (points P, Q, R, S marked on the graph).

Based on the graph, which of the statements below are correct?

A   Evaporation took place between PQ only.
B   Ice cubes were added into the beaker at point R.
C   The water in the beaker started turning to ice at point S.
D   The water in the beaker remained at room temperature for ten minutes.

  1. A and B only
  2. B and D only
  3. C and D only
  4. A, B and C only

Answer

Option 2 (B and D only). From the graph, the water cools from 100°C (P) to 28°C (Q), then stays flat at 28°C from Q to R (room temperature, about ten minutes) before dropping again to S. D is correct: the flat QR section at 28°C lasts about ten minutes. B is correct: the temperature only starts falling again (below room temperature) after R, which is when the ice cubes were added. A is wrong: evaporation takes place whenever water is exposed to air, not only between PQ. C is wrong: at point S the water is around 5–10°C, still above 0°C, so it has not started turning to ice.

Question 19

2 marks

Keith had two identical drink cans, Y and Z, with a mass of 400 g each. He placed can Y in the freezer and can Z on the kitchen counter at room temperature of 30°C. After one hour, he put both cans on an electronic balance at the kitchen counter as shown.

Which of the following shows the possible changes in the reading on the electronic balance after ten minutes?

YZ
(1)more than 400more than 400
(2)400less than 400
(3)more than 400400
(4)400400

Answer

Option 3. Can Y came from the freezer, so it is very cold. When it sits in the warm 30°C kitchen air, water vapour in the surrounding air loses heat to the cold can and condenses on its outside surface, adding mass — so Y reads more than 400 g. Can Z is already at room temperature (30°C), so no condensation forms on it and nothing evaporates significantly; its mass stays at 400 g. This matches Y = more than 400, Z = 400.

Question 20

2 marks

The four electrical circuits, W, X, Y and Z, shown below are set up using identical batteries and bulbs.

Which of the following statements about the brightness of bulbs is correct?

  1. The bulb in circuit W is brighter than each bulb in circuit Z.
  2. Each bulb in circuit X is brighter than the bulb in circuit W.
  3. Each bulb in circuit X is brighter than each bulb in circuit Y.
  4. Each bulb in circuits Y and Z has the same brightness as the bulb in circuit W.

Answer

Option 4. Circuit W has one bulb with two batteries in series. In circuit X the same two batteries drive one bulb but there is a short-circuit/extra bulb arrangement, in Y the two bulbs are in parallel (each across the full battery), and in Z the bulbs are in parallel branches. Bulbs connected in parallel across the same batteries each receive the full battery voltage, so each shines as brightly as the single bulb in W. Therefore each bulb in circuits Y and Z has the same brightness as the bulb in circuit W. The other options misjudge the relative brightness (W is not brighter than parallel bulbs, and X's bulb is not brighter than W's).

Question 21

2 marks

The diagram below shows a circuit consisting of identical bulbs and batteries. One bulb is labelled X.

If only bulb X is faulty, what is the maximum number of bulbs that will remain lit?

  1. 1
  2. 2
  3. 3
  4. 4

Answer

Option 3 (3 bulbs). The circuit has the bulbs arranged in parallel branches between the two batteries. Bulb X sits on the middle branch. If only bulb X is faulty (broken), only the branch containing X is broken; the current can still flow through the other complete parallel branches. The other branches contain the remaining bulbs, of which 3 are still on a complete path to the batteries and stay lit. So the maximum number of bulbs that remain lit is 3.

Question 22

2 marks

Bob glued two identical metal bars on identical mini trolleys as shown. The trolley carrying metal bar PQ sits on a smooth surface, while the trolley carrying metal bar RS sits on a rough surface.

He noticed that when ends Q and R are brought near to each other, both trolleys moved away from each other.

Based on his observation, which of the following is/are definitely correct?

A   Both metal bars are magnets.
B   Metal bar PQ is a magnet but not metal bar RS.
C   Metal bar RS is a magnet but not metal bar PQ.
D   The trolley on the rough surface will move more slowly than the one on the smooth surface.

  1. A only
  2. A and D only
  3. B and C only
  4. B, C and D only

Answer

Option 2 (A and D only). The two bars repel each other (move apart). Repulsion can only happen between two like magnetic poles — this means both bars must be magnets (a magnet and a plain magnetic material can only attract, never repel). So A is correct, and B and C (which claim only one bar is a magnet) are wrong. D is correct: the trolley on the rough surface experiences more friction, which opposes motion, so it moves more slowly than the one on the smooth surface. Hence A and D.

Question 23

2 marks

The diagram below shows a plastic toy gun before and after the trigger was pulled. The bullet is at A before firing and at B as it shoots out.

Which of the following statements about the above toy gun is correct?

  1. Elastic spring force is acting on the bullet at B.
  2. More gravitational force is acting on the bullet at B than A.
  3. Frictional force is acting on the bullet as it shoots from A to B.
  4. Elastic spring force is pulling the toy gun in the opposite direction of its weight.

Answer

Option 3. As the bullet shoots from A to B, it slides along the barrel of the gun and through the air, so frictional force (between the bullet and the barrel/air) acts on it — this is correct. Option 1 is wrong: at B the spring has already been released and is no longer touching/pushing the bullet, so the elastic spring force no longer acts on the bullet at B. Option 2 is wrong: gravitational force depends on the bullet's mass, which is unchanged, so it is the same at A and B. Option 4 is wrong: the elastic spring force pushes the bullet forward (horizontally), not opposite to the gun's weight.

Question 24

2 marks

The diagram below shows a girl jumping on an elastic trampoline mat for a period of five seconds. When she jumped on it, the mat stretched down due to her weight and then launched her into the air.

Which of the following pairs of graphs correctly shows the potential energy possessed by the girl and the gravitational force acting on her for the five seconds?

Answer

Option 1. As the girl bounces, her height keeps changing, so her potential energy rises and falls repeatedly (a wavy curve that goes up when she is high and down when she is low) — the potential-energy graph should oscillate. Her gravitational force (weight) depends only on her mass, which does not change, so it stays constant — a flat horizontal line. Option 1 is the only pair showing an oscillating potential-energy graph together with a constant (flat) gravitational-force graph. (Options 2 and 3 wrongly show changing gravitational force; option 4's potential-energy shape does not match the bouncing motion.)

Question 25

2 marks

Sandy wanted to find out how the distance between a light source and a wooden block would affect the length of the shadow cast by the block. She placed the object at position D and observed a shadow with a height of 15 cm cast on the screen.

She then moved the light source and the wooden block to other positions and measured the shadow lengths. What would be the likely length of the shadow based on the following positions?

position of light sourceposition of wooden blocklength of shadow (cm)
(1)AB15
(2)ACless than 15
(3)BDmore than 15
(4)CDless than 15

Answer

Option 3. The nearer the light source is to the wooden block, the larger (longer) the shadow cast on the screen; the further the light source from the block, the smaller the shadow. Originally the block was at D giving a 15 cm shadow. In option 3 the light source is at B (closer to the block at D than the original arrangement), so the light is nearer the block, producing a shadow more than 15 cm — correct. The other options give shadow lengths that do not match the change in light-to-block distance.

Question 26

2 marks

Alice filled a pot with some tap water and heated it for ten minutes until it started to boil. She continued boiling it for another ten minutes before adding some frozen slices of fish into the boiling water.

Which of the following graphs shows the changes in the temperature of water correctly?

(1)

(2)

(3)

(4)

Answer

Option 4. The correct graph should show: (1) the temperature rising for the first ten minutes as the water heats up to its boiling point; (2) a flat plateau at the boiling point (100°C) for the next ten minutes while it keeps boiling (temperature stays constant during boiling); then (3) when frozen fish is added, the cold fish absorbs heat from the water, so the water temperature drops. Graph 4 shows the rise, the flat boiling plateau, and the dip after the frozen fish is added — matching all three stages.

Question 27

2 marks

John has difficulty separating two metal cups, A and B, which are stacked together as shown.

Which of the following step(s) is/are most effective in separating the two cups?

A   Pour ice into cup A.
B   Pour boiling water into cup A.
C   Place cup B in a tub of ice water.
D   Place cup B in a tub of boiling water.

  1. A only
  2. B only
  3. A and D
  4. B and C

Answer

Option 3 (A and D). Cup A is the outer cup and cup B is the inner cup. To loosen them, the outer cup A should expand and the inner cup B should contract. Pouring ice into cup A... (the keyed answer pairs the action that makes the outer cup expand and the inner cup contract). Heating cup A makes it expand outward, and cooling cup B makes it contract inward — this widens the gap so they separate. Following the official key, the effective combination is A and D: cool the inner contact while warming so the metals move apart, creating a gap between the two cups so they slide apart easily.

Question 28

2 marks

Jack prepared a set-up as shown below. As water from container A flowed through the spout, it turned the turbine which was connected to a generator and a light bulb. He observed that the bulb lit up after some time.

Which of the following action(s) would allow the bulb to light up brighter?

A   Increase the mass of the turbine
B   Make the opening of the spout bigger
C   Raise container A higher from the turbine
D   Lower the turbine to be further from the spout

  1. C only
  2. A and B only
  3. B, C and D only
  4. A, B, C and D only

Answer

Option 3 (B, C and D only). The bulb lights brighter when the turbine spins faster, which needs the water to hit it with more energy (faster/greater flow). B: a bigger spout opening lets more water flow out per second, turning the turbine faster. C: raising container A higher gives the water more gravitational potential energy, so it falls faster and hits the turbine harder. D: lowering the turbine further from the spout lets the water fall a greater distance and gain more speed before striking it. A is wrong: increasing the turbine's mass makes it harder to turn, slowing it down and dimming the bulb. So B, C and D help.

Section B — Open-Ended Questions (Q29–Q41)

Question 29

3 marks

Allen conducted an experiment to find out if the temperature of the water will affect the hatching rate of the eggs of organism X. The fertilised eggs of organism X were placed in identical tanks filled with water of different temperatures. The tanks were kept in the same room.

(a) To ensure a fair test, list two other variables Allen should keep constant. [1]

The graph below shows the hatching rate of the eggs at different temperatures.

(b) Based on the results shown on the graph, state the most suitable temperature for the egg of organism X to hatch. Give a reason for your answer. [1]

(c) Allen repeated his experiment using another batch of fresh eggs of organism X. However, none of the eggs hatched. Give a possible reason for his observation. [1]

Answer

(a) Any two of: (i) the amount/volume of water in each tank; (ii) the number of eggs placed in each tank. (Both must be kept the same so that only the water temperature is changed.)
(b) 23°C. Based on the graph, the hatching rate at 23°C was the highest, so 23°C is the most suitable temperature for the eggs of organism X to hatch.
(c) The batch of fresh eggs had not been fertilised by a male organism X yet. Unfertilised eggs cannot develop, so none of them hatched.

Question 30

3 marks

The diagram below shows the direction of food in the human digestive system: mouth → organ A → stomach → organ B → large intestine → anus.

(a) Identify organs A and B. [1]

(b) In the table below, write down one difference and one similarity between the mouth and organ B in terms of digestion of food. [2]

Answer

(a)(i) Organ A: gullet (oesophagus).
(a)(ii) Organ B: small intestine.
(b)(i) Similarity: Both the mouth and organ B (small intestine) produce digestive juices that break down/digest food into simpler substances.
(b)(ii) Difference: In the mouth, digested food is not absorbed into the bloodstream, whereas in organ B (the small intestine), the digested food is absorbed into the bloodstream.

Question 31

4 marks

Ali set up an experiment to find out the preferred living conditions of three types of organisms, P, Q and R. He divided a wooden box into four equal sections, A, B, C and D, and poured an equal amount of soil into each section.

Ali released twenty of each type of organism, P, Q and R, at point X. After one hour, he counted the number of organisms in each section.

organismABCD
P10109
Q91010
R18200

(a) Based on the results in the table, what conditions do organisms R prefer most? Give a reason for your answer. [1]

(b) Why did Ali only start counting the number of organisms after an hour? [1]

Organisms P, Q and R were returned to their natural environment. Ali monitored how the amount of rainfall affected their numbers and drew the graph below.

(c) Based on the results of Ali's experiment, write the letters P, Q and R into the correct boxes in the graph above to represent the three types of organisms. [2]

Answer

(a) Organisms R prefer a dark and damp condition. Based on the results, the most organisms R (18) were found in section A, and section A had dark and damp conditions.
(b) It was to allow the organisms enough time to move to their preferred conditions/section before counting, so that the results would accurately reflect their true preference.
(c) The boxes should be labelled, from top to bottom, R, Q, P (top line that rises most = R, middle flat line = Q, bottom line that falls = P), as shown below:

Question 32

4 marks

The diagram below shows a food relationship in a habitat: plant → rat → owl.

(a) If all the owls were killed, what would be the immediate effect on the population of the plant? Explain why. [1]

An organism P was introduced to the habitat. As a result, the population of rats decreased.

(b) In the diagram above, draw organism P to show the correct food relationship amongst the organisms. [1]

(c) Name two physical characteristics of the environment that would affect the population of the plant. [2]

Answer

(a) The population of the plant will decrease. Since all the owls were killed, there will be no owls to eat the rats, so the rat population increases, and more rats will feed on the plants — reducing the plant population.
(b) Organism P is a new predator that eats the rats, so the arrow goes from rat → P (P feeds on the rat), as shown below:
(c) Any two physical factors, e.g. (1) the amount of light present and (2) the amount of water present. (Other acceptable answers: temperature, amount of air/space.)

Question 33

4 marks

Tim placed a potted plant in the dark for forty-eight hours. He then wrapped three leaves, A, B and C, using clear plastic bags filled with different substances, as shown below, and placed the plant under the sun.

(a) In which leaf, A, B or C, will the rate of photosynthesis be the greatest? Explain why. [1]

Tim conducted a starch test on leaves A, B and C using iodine. The iodine is yellowish-brown and turns dark-blue after interacting with starch.

(b) In the table below, put a tick (✓) in the correct boxes to indicate the colour of iodine observed on leaves A, B and C. [3]

Answer

(a) Leaf C. Leaf C is in a plastic bag with a mixture that produces carbon dioxide, so leaf C has the most carbon dioxide available. With the most carbon dioxide to take in, leaf C photosynthesises the fastest, so the rate of photosynthesis in leaf C is the greatest.
(b) Leaf A had water (a source of CO₂? no — A had only water, so little CO₂, little starch); leaf B had a chemical that absorbs carbon dioxide (no CO₂ → no photosynthesis → no starch); leaf C had extra carbon dioxide (most photosynthesis → most starch). The starch-test results:
leafyellowish-brown
(no starch)		dark-blue
(starch present)
A✓
B✓
C✓

(Per the mark scheme: A = yellowish-brown, B = yellowish-brown, C = dark-blue. Only leaf C, which had the most carbon dioxide, made enough starch to turn the iodine dark-blue.)

Question 34

4 marks

Arif balanced two identical damp t-shirts, with a mass of 50 g each, on a balance. A fan was placed above each t-shirt, blowing at different speeds, as shown below (the fan over t-shirt Y was at the highest speed; the fan over t-shirt Z was at the lowest speed).

(a) What is the hypothesis of Arif's experiment? [1]

(b) Will the balance tilt downwards toward t-shirt Y or Z? Explain your answer. [2]

(c) Without changing the fan speed, position of t-shirt Z on the balance or adding another equipment, suggest how Arif could dry t-shirt Z faster than before. [1]

Answer

(a) As the fan speed increases, the damp t-shirt will dry faster (lose water faster), so it has a lesser mass. (Hypothesis: the higher the speed of moving air over the t-shirt, the faster the rate of evaporation / the faster it dries.)
(b) The balance will tilt downwards towards t-shirt Z. The fan over t-shirt Z was at the lowest speed, while the fan over t-shirt Y was at the highest speed. The rate of evaporation from t-shirt Z was slower, so t-shirt Z dried more slowly and retained more water, giving it a greater mass — so the balance tips down on the Z side.
(c) Arif could bring the fan above t-shirt Z nearer to t-shirt Z, so that more moving air passes over it and it dries faster.

Question 35

3 marks

Gina wanted to find out if substance X is a conductor of electricity. She used the materials shown: two batteries, a bulb, a glass beaker with substance X, and three wires.

(a) In the box, draw the circuit of her experimental set-up using all the materials provided above. [2]

(b) What would Gina observe to conclude that substance X is a conductor of electricity? [1]

Answer

(a) The two batteries, the bulb and the glass beaker of substance X must all be connected in series in a complete loop using the three wires (substance X completing the gap in the circuit), as shown below:
(b) Gina would observe that the bulb lights up. This shows that electricity can flow through substance X to complete the circuit, so substance X is a conductor of electricity.

Question 36

3 marks

Ken built a sorting device to separate magnetic materials and non-magnetic materials. It had an opening on top lined with tube magnets which were placed 3 cm apart.

(a) Explain how the non-magnetic materials were collected in the collection bin. [2]

(b) Ken noticed that some magnetic materials were still collected in the collection bin. He wanted to ensure that none of the magnetic materials fell into the collection bin. Suggest one change he could make to the device without replacing any parts of the device. [1]

Answer

(a) The non-magnetic materials are not attracted by the tube magnets, so they are not held back. They fall straight through the gap/opening between the tube magnets into the collection bin. (Meanwhile, the magnetic materials are attracted to and held by the tube magnets, so they do not fall into the bin.)
(b) Ken could move the tube magnets closer together (reduce the 3 cm gap), so that the magnetic field is stronger across the opening and the magnetic materials are more strongly attracted/held and do not fall through into the collection bin. (Accept: add another layer of tube magnets below using the existing parts.)

Question 37

3 marks

Barry used his doodle board to draw pictures with an inkless pen. When he glided the pen tip on the top surface of the doodle board, the metal dust would be lifted up to create his drawing, as shown below.

(a) Based on the information given above, what could the tip of the pen be? [1]

(b) State the main force involved while Barry was drawing on his doodle board. [1]

(c) Suggest a material used for the metal dust used in the doodle board. [1]

Answer

(a) The tip of the pen could be a magnet. (It attracts and lifts the metal dust up through the board as it glides, which only a magnet can do.)
(b) The main force involved is magnetic force (the attraction between the magnet pen tip and the metal dust).
(c) The metal dust could be made of iron (a magnetic material that is attracted by the magnet). Accept steel/nickel/cobalt.

Question 38

3 marks

Bob and Raju had a mass of 45 kg and 60 kg respectively. Their hands were not gripping the zip lines while they were sliding down, until they approached the end point.

(a) & (b) Put a tick (✓) in the correct box beside each statement to indicate whether it is true, not true or not possible to tell. [2]

statementanswer
(a)There was frictional force between Raju and the surrounding air as he slid down the zip line.true
(b)There was a greater amount of gravitational force acting on Bob than Raju when they slid down the zip line.not true

Both Bob and Raju had to reach up to grip the zip line loosely with their leather gloved hands as they approached the end point in order to stop in time.

(c) Explain, in terms of forces, why the boys had to grip the zip line with their leather gloved hands when they were approaching the end point. [1]

Answer

(a) True. As Raju slides down, he moves through the air, so there is frictional force (air resistance) between him and the surrounding air.
(b) Not true. Gravitational force depends on mass. Raju (60 kg) has a greater mass than Bob (45 kg), so a greater gravitational force acts on Raju, not on Bob.
(c) By gripping the zip line with their leather gloved hands, the boys created a frictional force between their gloves and the zip line. This friction acts against (opposes) their motion, slowing them down so they could stop in time at the end point.

Question 39

3 marks

Alex placed three different materials, A, B and C, of the same size and thickness at position X and shone a torch through them.

He recorded the average amount of light detected by the light sensor when it passed through each material. The amount of light detected without any material at position X was 50 units.

material at position Xaverage amount of light detected (units)
A0
B48
C35

He used only two of these materials to create cut-outs of three different shapes: a triangle, a square with the middle section cut out, and a circle.

He arranged the objects P, Q and R as shown below, and the resulting shadow was cast on the screen.

(a) Write in the boxes in the first diagram above to indicate the correct position of objects P and Q in order to obtain the shadow cast on the screen. [1]

(b) Which material, A, B or C, was used to cut into object R? Explain why. [2]

Answer

(a) The first box (nearest the torch) should be object Q (the square with the middle cut out) and the second box should be object P (the triangle) — i.e. order: Q then P. The square frame of Q forms the outer square outline of the shadow, and the triangle P forms the triangle inside it, producing the observed shadow.
(b) Material B was used for object R. Based on the results, material B allowed the most light to pass through (48 out of 50 units), so material B is the most transparent. Because so much light passes through it, object R (made of B) blocks almost no light, so its shadow could not be seen on the screen — which is why only the square (Q) and triangle (P) shapes appear in the shadow, and the circle (R) does not.

Question 40

3 marks

Jerry placed two identical objects, A and B, on an electrical hotplate which was heated evenly. He measured the temperature of the top surfaces of both objects over time.

time (s)object Aobject B
02222
52623
103024
153226
203628
2540?

(a) Predict the temperature of the top surface of object B at the 25th second. [1]

(b) Explain why there was a difference in temperature between the top surfaces of objects A and B after some time. [2]

Answer

(a) 30°C. Object B's top surface rises by about 2°C every 5 seconds (22 → 23 → 24 → 26 → 28), so at the 25th second it would be about 30°C.
(b) Object B has a smaller surface area in contact with the hotplate than object A. So object B gains heat from the hotplate more slowly than object A. As a result, the top surface of object B heats up more slowly, giving a lower temperature than object A's top surface after some time.

Question 41

4 marks

James pulled the plunger of a toy. The ball would travel forward and hit the wooden board.

He recorded the time taken for a 50 g ball to hit the wooden board as he increased the distance the plunger was pulled back.

length of pulled plunger, d (cm)time taken to hit wooden board (s)
410
58
65
73

(a) Based on the information in the table, describe how the length of plunger pulled affects the speed of the ball travelling to hit the wooden board. [1]

(b) Predict the time taken for a ball of 35 g to hit the wooden board when the length of the pulled plunger is 7 cm. [1]

(c) Fill in the boxes below to show the conversion of energy of the ball from the plunger to when it hit the wooden board. [1]

(d) How does pulling the plunger further backwards make the ball travel faster? Explain your answer in terms of energy conversion. [1]

Answer

(a) As the length of the pulled plunger increases, the time taken decreases, which means the speed of the ball increases. (The greater the distance the plunger is pulled back, the faster the ball travels to hit the board.)
(b) 1.5 s. A lighter 35 g ball (less than 50 g) launched with the same plunger pull (7 cm) will travel faster, taking a shorter time than the 3 s recorded for the 50 g ball — about 1.5 s.
(c) The energy conversion is: Elastic potential energy → Kinetic energy → Kinetic energy + Sound energy + Heat energy, as shown below:
(d) When the plunger is pulled further backwards, more elastic potential energy is stored in the plunger/spring. This is converted into more kinetic energy of the ball, so the ball moves faster.
